content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 108r–109v, LC PDF pages 214–215 | **Source Images:** DEU_017_page_001.jpg, DEU_017_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0154 — DEU_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** You shall not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep in which there is a blemish, any evil thing, for that is an abomination [to'evah] to the LORD your God.

**2** If there is found in your midst, in one of your gates which the LORD your God is giving you, a man or a woman who does what is evil in the eyes of the LORD your God, by transgressing his covenant—

**3** and goes and serves other gods and bows down to them, or to the sun or to the moon or to any of the host of heaven, which I have not commanded—

**4** and it is told to you and you hear it, and you inquire thoroughly; and behold, it is true—the thing is established—this abomination was done in Yisra'el—

**5** then you shall bring out that man or that woman who did this evil thing to your gates, the man or the woman, and you shall stone them with stones, and they shall die.

**6** By the mouth of two witnesses or three witnesses shall the one who dies be put to death; he shall not be put to death by the mouth of one witness.

**7** The hand of the witnesses shall be upon him first to put him to death, and afterward the hand of all the people; and you shall burn out the evil from your midst.

**8** If a matter is too difficult for you in judgment—between blood and blood, between plea and plea, between stroke and stroke, matters of dispute in your gates—then you shall arise and go up to the place which the LORD your God will choose.

**9** And you shall come to the Levitical priests and to the judge who will be in those days, and you shall inquire, and they shall declare to you the word of the judgment.

**10** And you shall do according to the word that they declare to you from that place which the LORD will choose, and you shall keep and do according to all that they instruct you.

**11** According to the instruction [torah] that they instruct you and according to the judgment that they tell you, you shall do; you shall not turn aside from the word that they declare to you, to the right or to the left.

**12** And the man who acts presumptuously, so as not to listen to the priest who stands to minister there before the LORD your God, or to the judge—that man shall die; and you shall burn out the evil from Yisra'el.

**13** And all the people shall hear and fear and shall not act presumptuously anymore.

**14** When you come to the land which the LORD your God is giving you and you possess it and dwell in it, and you say, "I will set a king over me, like all the nations that are around me"—

**15** you shall surely set over you a king whom the LORD your God will choose; from the midst of your brothers you shall set a king over you; you may not put a foreigner [ish nokri] over you, who is not your brother.

**16** Only he must not multiply horses [lo yarbeh-lo susim] for himself, and he must not cause the people to return to Egypt in order to multiply horses, inasmuch as the LORD has said to you, "You shall never return that way again."

**17** And he must not multiply wives for himself [lo yarbeh-lo nashim], so that his heart does not turn away; and silver and gold he must not greatly multiply for himself.

**18** And it shall be, when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself a copy of this instruction [mishneh ha-torah ha-zot] in a scroll, from before the Levitical priests.

**19** And it shall be with him and he shall read in it all the days of his life, so that he may learn to fear the LORD his God, to keep all the words of this instruction and these statutes, to do them—

**20** so that his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers, and so that he does not turn aside from the commandment to the right or to the left, so that he may prolong [days] in his kingdom, he and his sons, in the midst of Yisra'el.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — to'evah:** "Abomination." The chapter bridges from the festival/justice law of ch. 16 to new sections, beginning with a sacrificial integrity clause. The blemished animal principle from ch. 15 is restated.

**v.2 — la'avor et-berito:** "By transgressing his covenant." The idolater is defined first as a covenant-breaker (la'avor = to transgress/cross over). The covenant framework (berit) is the backbone of Deuteronomic theology.

**v.3 — le-shemesh o la-yare'ach o le-khol-tseva ha-shamayim:** "To the sun or to the moon or to any of the host of heaven." Astral worship — the most explicit list of forbidden celestial cults in Deuteronomy. The phrase asher lo tsiviti, "which I have not commanded," is the divine first-person aside.

**v.6 — al-pi shenayim edim o shloshah edim:** "By the mouth of two witnesses or three witnesses." The juridical principle of multiple witnesses — no capital sentence on a single witness. LXX (p. 154) ἐπὶ δυσὶν μάρτυσιν ἢ ἐπὶ τρισὶν — exact agreement.

**v.8 — ben-dam le-dam, ben-din le-din, u-ven-nega le-nega:** "Between blood and blood, between plea and plea, between stroke and stroke." Three pairs of difficult distinctions: homicide cases (blood), civil litigation (din = plea/case), and bodily injury (nega = stroke/plague). The triadic structure is unique in Torah law. LXX ἀνὰ μέσον αἵματος καὶ αἵματος... — preserves the tripling.

**v.9 — ha-kohanim ha-levi'im:** "The Levitical priests." The compound designation (kohanim ha-levi'im) is characteristic of Deuteronomy — merging priestly and Levitical identity in ways that differ from the Priestly code of Leviticus/Numbers.

**v.11 — torah asher yorukha:** "Instruction that they instruct you" — cognate infinitive absolute in the Hiphil: yorukha from yarah, "to teach/instruct." The root of torah = yarah. EMET preserves "instruction" throughout. LXX τὸν νόμον.

**v.12 — ha-ish asher ya'aseh be-zadon:** "The man who acts presumptuously." Zadon (זָדוֹן) = presumptuousness/arrogance — the deliberate, defiant act as opposed to an inadvertent error (shegagah). The death penalty applies to deliberate flouting of judicial authority.

**v.15 — la-som 'alekha melekh:** "You shall surely set over you a king." The royal law (mishpat ha-melekh) anticipates kingship rather than commanding it — it is permission with restriction. The king must be chosen by the LORD and must be a brother (Israelite). LXX ἐκλέξεις ἐπίστησαι.

**v.16 — lo yarbeh-lo susim:** "He must not multiply horses for himself." Three lo yarbeh ("he must not multiply") prohibitions: horses (v.16), wives (v.17a), silver and gold (v.17b). The horses-Egypt connection is explicit — Solomonic fulfillment of all three prohibitions (1 Kgs 10-11) is the canonical subtext. LXX (p. 154) οὐ πληθυνεῖ ἵππους — exact.

**v.17 — ve-lo yashiv et-ha'am mitzraymah:** "He must not cause the people to return to Egypt." Return to Egypt is specifically prohibited as the rationale for the horse-limit. The horse trade ran through Egypt.

**v.18 — mishneh ha-torah ha-zot:** "A copy of this instruction." Mishneh (מִשְׁנֶה) means "a duplicate/copy" or "second." This is the phrase from which the LXX coined Deuteronomion = "second law," and thus the book's name. EMET renders it literally: "a copy of this instruction." The king's personal Torah scroll — to be read daily — is a political theology in miniature. LXX τὸ δευτερονόμιον τοῦτο — "this Deuteronomy."

**v.19 — ve-qara vo kol-yeme chayyav:** "And he shall read in it all the days of his life." The king's daily Torah reading is meant to produce yir'ah (fear of God) and humility before his brothers. The text redefines kingship: the king is not above the law but its most dedicated student.

**v.20 — le-vilti rum-levavo me'echav:** "So that his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers." The royal temptation — hubris toward fellow Israelites — is the primary concern. The king remains a brother. LXX ἵνα μὴ ὑψωθῇ ἡ καρδία αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀδελφῶν.

**LXX comparison (Vaticanus p. 154):** Vaticanus p. 154 contains ch. 17 within the broader spread. The text closely follows the MT. Most significant: v.18, LXX reads τὸ δευτερονόμιον τοῦτο ("this Deuteronomy") for mishneh ha-torah — the LXX has interpreted "copy of this instruction" as "this second law/Deuteronomy," which gave the entire book its Greek name. This is the most consequential translation choice in the whole LXX of Deuteronomy.

— *EMET*
